metadata.summary: Workspace/runtime separation for solo VPS: source in ~/workspace/repos/, runtime in /srv/project/. Deploy via rsync, rollback by git checkout + redeploy. Git worktrees enable parallel agent work.

content.title: Git Server Workflow
content.summary: Workspace/runtime separation pattern for solo VPS deployments: source code lives in `~/workspace/repos/`, services run from `/srv/project/`. Deploy with rsync (not `git pull` to runtime). Rollback by checking out a previous commit in the workspace repo and redeploying. Git worktrees enable parallel agent work on the same repository without branch conflicts.

why.p: Running `git pull` in the runtime directory mixes git operations with a running service — branch switches can break live processes, and uncommitted changes interfere with pulls. rsync copies only changed files in milliseconds, keeping the runtime clean and reproducible. The workspace/runtime split also makes rollback trivial: the workspace is a full git history; the runtime is just synced source.

list.item: Solo VPS with multiple services (Python/Node apps + shared SDK)
Need to deploy from workspace to server without CI/CD
Need quick rollback (`git checkout HEAD~1 && deploy.sh service`)
Running multiple parallel Claude Code agents on the same repo (worktrees)
Push-to-deploy via post-receive hook on a bare server repo
Multi-server deployments — rsync from one machine to many is unwieldy; use Ansible or Capistrano
When the team uses Docker for the application (use image tags + `docker compose up` instead)
Monorepos where workspace and runtime are the same directory (no separation needed)

section.name: core-patterns
section.p: Workspace/runtime separation layout, rsync deploy flags, dependency detection, and multi-repo deploy order.

rule.statement: Keep source code in `~/workspace/repos/` and deployed runtime in `/srv/project/`. Never run git operations (checkout, pull, merge) in the runtime directory.
rule.rationale: Git operations in the runtime directory can interrupt running services, change files mid-request, and leave the directory in a partially-updated state.

example.title: Directory layout: workspace and runtime separation
example.code:
~/workspace/repos/nero-core/ # git repo, development ~/workspace/repos/nero-sdk/ # shared library (source) ~/workspace/.env # master env file (chmod 600) ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh # deploy orchestrator
/srv/nero/nero-core/ # systemd runs from here src/ # rsync'd from workspace .venv/ # managed by deploy script .env -> ~/workspace/.env # symlink /srv/nero/nero-sdk/ # shared library (deployed)

rule.statement: Use `rsync -a --delete` with explicit excludes for `.git/`, `__pycache__/`, `.venv/`, `.env`, and `node_modules/`. Never rsync these directories to the runtime.
rule.rationale: `--delete` removes files from runtime that no longer exist in workspace. Without excludes it would delete the `.venv` managed separately and expose secrets via `.env`.

rule.statement: Deploy shared libraries (SDK, shared models) before services that depend on them. Define an explicit ordered array in the deploy script.
rule.rationale: If a service restarts before the SDK it imports is updated, it starts against the old library code, causing version mismatches until the next restart.

example.title: Multi-repo deploy order
example.code: # Correct order: SDK → infra → services → frontend DEPLOY_ORDER=(nero-sdk nero-infra nero-core nero-channel-web nero-channel-tg nero-web)

rule.statement: Avoid reinstalling pip dependencies on every deploy. Compare requirements files between workspace and last deployed state.
rule.rationale: Dependency installs add several seconds to every deploy. Checking if requirements changed is instant and can skip the install step in 90% of deploys.

example.title: Dependency change detection
example.code: # Install only if requirements changed if ! diff -q "$src/requirements.txt" "$dst/.last-requirements.txt" &>/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "Requirements changed, installing..." "$dst/.venv/bin/pip" install -q -r "$dst/requirements.txt" cp "$dst/requirements.txt" "$dst/.last-requirements.txt" fi

section.name: rollback-and-worktrees
section.p: Git-based rollback procedure, tagged releases, worktree commands for parallel agents, and post-receive hook pattern.

rule.statement: To roll back a service, check out the previous commit (or a tag) in the workspace repo, then re-run the deploy script. Never manually edit files in the runtime directory.
rule.rationale: Rolling back via the deploy script ensures the runtime is brought to a consistent state: correct source files, correct pip dependencies, and a clean systemd restart. Ad-hoc manual edits in the runtime diverge from the workspace and break the next normal deploy.

example.title: Rollback to previous commit or tag
example.code:
# Roll back nero-core to the previous commit cd ~/workspace/repos/nero-core git checkout HEAD~1 bash ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh nero-core
# Roll back to a specific tag git checkout v1.4.2 bash ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh nero-core
# Return to latest after investigation git checkout main bash ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh nero-core

rule.statement: Tag every production release with `git tag v<major>.<minor>.<patch>` before deploying. Push tags to origin.
rule.rationale: Untagged history requires `git log` forensics to find a safe rollback point. Tags make `git checkout v1.4.1` instant and unambiguous. Tags are also visible in `git log --oneline --decorate`.

example.title: Tagging releases
example.code: git tag v1.5.0 git push origin v1.5.0 bash ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh all

rule.statement: Use `git worktree add` to give each parallel agent its own working directory on a separate branch. Never have two agents operate on the same branch simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Two agents on the same branch will overwrite each other's uncommitted changes, cause merge conflicts on push, and produce interleaved commits that are hard to review. Each worktree has its own index and HEAD, so agents are fully isolated at the git level.

example.title: Worktree creation for parallel agents
example.code:
# Create a worktree for an agent task git worktree add ../nero-core-agent-1 -b agent/task-123 # Agent 1 works in ../nero-core-agent-1/
git worktree add ../nero-core-agent-2 -b agent/task-456 # Agent 2 works in ../nero-core-agent-2/
# List active worktrees git worktree list
# Remove after merging git worktree remove ../nero-core-agent-1 git branch -d agent/task-123

section.note: Claude Code uses worktrees automatically when the `worktrees` feature is enabled in `.claude/settings.json`. The pattern above is for manual agent orchestration or scripts.

rule.statement: On a bare repo server, use a `post-receive` hook to trigger deploys automatically when `main` is pushed. The hook must be idempotent and log output to a file.
rule.rationale: Manual SSH + pull cycles after every push are error-prone and slow. A post-receive hook runs once per push, on the correct branch, with no human step required. Logging to a file allows asynchronous inspection of deploy output without holding the git push connection open.

example.title: Post-receive hook for push-to-deploy
example.code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash # ~/repos/nero-core.git/hooks/post-receive DEPLOY_LOG=~/workspace/logs/deploy-nero-core.log WORKSPACE=~/workspace/repos/nero-core
while read oldrev newrev refname; do branch="${refname#refs/heads/}" if [[ "$branch" == "main" ]]; then echo "[post-receive] Deploying nero-core @ $newrev" cd "$WORKSPACE" || exit 1 git fetch origin main git reset --hard origin/main bash ~/workspace/deploy/deploy.sh nero-core >> "$DEPLOY_LOG" 2>&1 echo "[post-receive] Done. Log: $DEPLOY_LOG" fi done

antipattern.description: Running `git pull` inside the post-receive hook without `--ff-only`.
antipattern.reason: A non-fast-forward pull triggers an interactive merge or rebase prompt, which hangs the hook indefinitely because there is no TTY. Use `git fetch` + `git reset --hard origin/main` instead.

rule.statement: Use `main` as the single deployable branch. Feature work goes on short-lived branches merged via PR. Never deploy from feature branches to production.
rule.rationale: Deploying from arbitrary feature branches creates ambiguity about what is running in production. A single deployable branch (`main`) means the deploy script always knows what to check out, and the post-receive hook has a single trigger condition.

template.purpose: Master deploy script: rsync + venv + systemd restart, supports all and --rebuild-venv
Rollback a service to a previous commit or tag and redeploy
Bare repo post-receive hook for push-to-deploy on main branch
